story:
Jennie has spent years drifting through the same recurring dream: a man with a hazy face takes care of her, buys her favorite coffee, and gives her the kind of affection she has always longed for. She tells herself he must be the one meant for her—her soulmate, her twin fire. And every time the dream leaves her aching with hope, she tells Von, her best friend and the person she trusts most. Von listens, steady and kind, reminding her that dreams might be signs or might simply be her subconscious shaping what she wants. If she never meets the man from her dreams, he says gently, it might have been only that—a dream.

Then one day, the dream collides with reality. Jennie blinks, startled, as Von stands in front of her with a warm smile and a steaming cup of her favorite coffee, just the way she likes it. Her heart stutters. “Von…?” she whispers. He laughs softly, teasing that she looks like she’s seen a ghost. In that heartbeat, confusion and recognition crash through her. The tenderness. The attention to small details. The safety she always feels around him. How had she never let herself see it?

She had always protected their bond, guarding it from anything that could break it. Von had been her constant—for jokes, for late-night talks, for quiet company through storms. She had filed those fluttering moments away, labeling them “friendship,” believing that the love in her dreams must be something separate, something grander and new. But as the shock fades, the truth steps forward: maybe her dreams weren’t prophecies at all—maybe they were her heart nudging her toward what was already there.

Von reaches up and brushes a strand of hair behind her ear, and a shiver races down her spine. His voice is careful but brave. He tells her he has liked her for the longest time. The scenes she described to him, all those mornings and gestures and feelings, weren’t foreign to him; they were echoes of his own quiet hopes. She asks why he never said anything. He admits he was afraid—afraid of ruining what they had, of losing her altogether. So he stayed the loyal friend, supporting her as she searched for someone who looked like a dream when everything she wanted was next to her.

Tears rise and spill as Jennie understands the weight he has been carrying and the way she has been running in circles around something simple and true. In that moment, the faceless man in her dreams sharpens into someone familiar. It was Von all along—the one who knows her inside out, who has seen her at her highest and lowest and stayed, loving her anyway. She reaches for him, threading her fingers through his, and finally says the words she’s been afraid to face: she likes him too, more than she can express.

Relief and joy break over his features as he pulls her into an embrace. The puzzle pieces click into place—no magic, no mystery—just two people who finally meet each other where they have always been. Jennie realizes she has found her twin fire, her soulmate, in the person she least expected and most needed: the best friend who had loved her all along.
